content:
Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes Koichi Takada a,b,*, Akinori Okada a, Hiroshi Hirano a , Joji Kadota a, Tooru Kitagawa b,c, Hideto Matsuyama c,d , Tomohisa Yoshioka b,c, Keizo Nakagawa b,c,** a Research Division of Materials Science and Engineering, Osaka Research Institute of Industrial Science and Technology, 1-6-50, Morinomiya, Joto, Osaka, 536-8553, Japanb Graduate School of Science, Technology, and Innovation, Kobe University, 1-1 Rokkodai, Nada, Kobe, 657-8501, Japanc Research Center for Membrane and Film Technology, Kobe University, 1-1 Rokkodai, Nada, Kobe, 657-8501, Japand Department of Chemical Science and Engineering, Kobe University, 1-1 Rokkodai, Nada, Kobe, 657-8501, Japan

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Poly(lactic acid)Branched structureUltrafiltration membraneNonsolvent-induced phase separationBiodegradable polymer

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: A B S T R A C T Poly(lactic acid) (PLA) is a biomass-derived biodegradable polymer that shows promise as a circular resource for water-treatment membranes. The molecular structure of PLA affects its phase separation behavior, enabling control of pore formation to improve membrane performance. Changing the molecular chain density of PLA also affects its biodegradation behavior. However, conventional polymerization methods hardly provide PLA with both narrow molecular-weight distribution and high molecular weight, which limits research on the molecular structure of PLA. In this study, linear and star-branched PLA with four or eight arms were synthesized via organocatalyzed ring-opening polymerization, achieving a weight-average molecular weight (Mw) of ~95,000 and narrow dispersity (≤1.2). PLA membranes were prepared via nonsolvent-induced phase separation and the effects of branch number and Mw on pore structure and hydrolysis behavior were investigated. The membrane surfaces exhibited fine pores with underlying finger-like macrovoids. Molecular weight cut-off increased with branch number (linear: 15,000 Da; four: 17,000 Da; eight: 52,000 Da). This trend was attributed to the decrease in solution viscosity with increasing branch number, which facilitated phase separation and pore growth. The decrease rate of molecular weight in accelerated hydrolysis tests (pH 9, 65 ◦C, 215 h) was 90 % for four-arm PLA, 70 % for linear, and 49 % for eight-arm PLA.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: rate of molecular weight in accelerated hydrolysis tests (pH 9, 65 ◦C, 215 h) was 90 % for four-arm PLA, 70 % for linear, and 49 % for eight-arm PLA. A moderate PLA branching structure promoted hydrolysis, whereas an excessive branch number increased the chain density near the branch points, inhibiting water penetration and hydrolysis. These findings indicate that branch number, Mw, and viscosity control are important for improving membrane performance and degradability.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: 1. Introduction Ultrafiltration (UF) membranes are widely used because of their low energy consumption, compact system footprint, and high reliability in terms of product purity compared with the performance of other con­ventional separation methods. Materials used for membranes are often petroleum-derived and non-biodegradable polymers such as poly­ethylene [1,2], polysulfone [3,4], and poly(vinylidene fluoride) [5,6]. UF and microfiltration (MF) membranes composed of these materials are used for drinking water treatment, wastewater treatment, industrial water purification, and in food and beverage industries [7]. Biomass-derived and/or biodegradable polymers are favorable mate­rials for limiting carbon dioxide emissions and preventing waste from remaining in the environment. Representative examples include poly (butylene succinate) (PBS), poly(3-hydroxybutyrate-co-3-hydroxy hexanoate), and poly(lactic acid) (PLA), the latter of which has ach­ieved the most extensive industrial application [8]. PLA is a circular resource-type material that does not biodegrade in water at ordinary temperatures, which makes it promising for use in water-treatment membranes [7]. PLA MF and UF membranes prepared via phase

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: Available online 29 January 2026 0376-7388/ 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/bync-nd/4.0/ ). separation have been reported. For example, Tanaka et al. demonstrated PLA MF membranes with bacterial removal capability and investigated how the preparation process influenced membrane structure and per­formance [9–11]. Moriya and colleagues reported hollow-fiber PLA UF membranes, in which the addition of polyethylene glycol (PEG)–PLA copolymers imparted antifouling properties [12,13]. Other studies highlighted the effects of coagulation bath composition and the PLA concentration of casting solution on membrane structure [14,15]. In the development of PLA-based membranes, two major points need to be considered: molecular structure and biodegradability. The mo­lecular structure of PLA controls membrane structure and performance. However, previous studies have been limited to stereocomplex PLA, which is composed of Dand L-enantiomers [16,17], or copolymers of PLA with other polymers [18]. In addition, PLAs used in previous studies have been linear; the effect of a branched structure on phase separation structure has not been investigated. The reasons are follows: 1. commercially available PLA only has linear molecular structure; PLA with controlled molecular structures is not available; 2.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: reasons are follows: 1. commercially available PLA only has linear molecular structure; PLA with controlled molecular structures is not available; 2. conventional tin-based catalysts commonly used for PLA synthesis tend to induce side reactions that generate linear chains, making it difficult to obtain branched PLA with high purity; 3. the side reactions broaden molecular weight distribution [19].These reasons make it difficult to distinguish the effects of molecular weight and branch structure on phase separation structure. Although catalysts that suppress side reactions have been reported [20,21], the molecular weights achieved are generally around 10,000, which is insufficient to impart the mechanical strength required for the porous structures of membranes. Biodegradation of PLA membranes is another consideration. Con­ventional linear PLA typically takes a long time to degrade, and thus accelerating its decomposition after use is desirable. PLA degradation proceeds in two stages: initial chemical hydrolysis, which decreases molecular weight, followed by microbial assimilation once the arm chain length of PLA decreases to below about 10,000 Da [22,23]. The initial hydrolysis strongly accelerates above the glass transition tem­perature (Tg), yet even then, substantial time is required before the onset of microbial degradation.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: ongly accelerates above the glass transition tem­perature (Tg), yet even then, substantial time is required before the onset of microbial degradation. Although copolymerization with other biodegradable monomers has been investigated to address this issue, it often results in an undesirable decrease of Tg because many biode­gradable polymers exhibit low Tg [24]. PLA has a Tg of 50–60 ◦C, and thus hydrolysis does not progress easily under general water-treatment conditions (below 40 ◦C). However, if copolymerization decreases Tg below the operating temperature, there is a risk accelerating undesired degradation during use. Introducing a branched structure into PLA has been proposed as a strategy to accelerate degradation. This approach does not decrease Tg [25,26], and therefore it is considered that branched PLA will be suitable for use in water-treatment membranes. Our research group has developed an organocatalyst capable of suppressing side reactions, enabling the synthesis of star-branched PLA with high molecular weight and narrow molecular weight distribution [27,28]. These PLAs possess the strength required for use as membranes because of their high molecular weight and make it possible to investi­gate the relationship between molecular structure (e.g., branch number) or molecular weight and membrane structure. In this study, we examine how the primary structure of PLA (molecular structure or molecular weight) affects its pore structure (phase separation structure).

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: study, we examine how the primary structure of PLA (molecular structure or molecular weight) affects its pore structure (phase separation structure). Linear and star-branched PLAs with different branch number and molecular weight are synthesized by ring-opening polymerization of lactide. Then, membranes are prepared via nonsolvent-induced phase separation (NIPS). Membrane structure and performance are evaluated to investi­gate their relationship with the primary structure of PLA. Four-arm star-branched PLAs with different molecular weight are studied to investigate the effect of molecular weight on membrane structure and performance. Hydrolysis of these membranes is investigated by measuring the time-dependent change of molecular weight in acceler­ated hydrolysis tests. The relationship between molecular structure and hydrolysis behavior is analyzed. This investigation covers not only the production and use of PLA UF membranes but also their disposal to assess their environmental impact after use. 2. Experimental section 2.1. Materials L-Lactide (L-LA), pentaerythritol (PeOH), N,N′-diisopropylcarbodii­mide (DIPCI), p-toluenesulfonic acid, lithium bromide (LiBr), and so­dium azide (NaN3) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Tokyo, Japan).

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: dii­mide (DIPCI), p-toluenesulfonic acid, lithium bromide (LiBr), and so­dium azide (NaN3) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Tokyo, Japan). 4-Dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), dichloromethane (CH2Cl2; stabilized with 2-methyl-2-butene), tetrahydrofuran (THF; stabilized with 2,6-di-t-butyl-4-methylphenol), deuterated chloroform (CDCl3; stabilized with silver foil and 0.003 % tetramethylsilane), PEG 600, and PEG 6000 were purchased from Nacalai Tesque, Inc. (Kyoto, Japan). L-Lactic acid, ethylene glycol, molecular sieves 3A, molecular sieves 4A, 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), and PEG 100,000 were purchased from Fujifilm Wako Pure Chemical Corporation (Osaka, Japan). Tri­fluoromethanesulfonic acid (TfOH), tripentaerythritol (TriPeOH), PEG 10,000, PEG 20,000, and PEG 35,000 were purchased from SigmaAldrich Japan (Tokyo, Japan). Easi Vial PEG/PEO was purchased from Agilent Technologies, Inc. (Santa Clara, CA, USA). L-LA was recrystal­lized three times from toluene and stored under nitrogen at 4 ◦C. DMAP was purified by sublimation and stored under nitrogen. All other ma­terials were used as received. 2.2. Ring-opening polymerization of L-LA Linear PLA with hydroxyl groups at both ends (HO-PLA-OH), linear PLA with a carboxylic acid group at one end and hydroxyl group at the other end (HOOC-PLA-OH), and star-branched PLA were synthesized in accordance with previously reported methods [27,28].

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: end and hydroxyl group at the other end (HOOC-PLA-OH), and star-branched PLA were synthesized in accordance with previously reported methods [27,28]. In this paper, four-arm star-branched PLA with a weight-average molecular weight (Mw) of 100,000 is designated as 4S-PLA-100k according to its branch number and Mw. A typical polymerization procedure was conducted as follows. A 20-mL Schlenk flask was flame-dried under vacuum and then DMAP (1 mol%), 4-dimethylaminopyridinium trifluoromethanesulfonate (DMAP⋅HOTf; 1 mol%), L-LA (10 mmol), and initiator alcohol (0.07–0.50 mmol) were successively added. The mixture was stirred under nitrogen at 110 ◦C for 45–120 min (detail conditions are shown in Table 1). The reaction product was dissolved in CH2Cl2 (7 mL). The resulting solution was added dropwise into cold methanol (300 mL) to give a white precipitate. The obtained crude polymer was dried under vacuum at 40 ◦C for 5 h. To purify the crude polymer to obtain star-branched PLAs (4S-PLA50k, 4S-PLA-65k, 4S-PLA-100k, and 8S-PLA-100k), it was dissolved in THF and then methanol was slowly added dropwise until a precipitate formed. The fractions with high molecular weight were collected and then dried under vacuum at 40 ◦C for 6 h before use in subsequent experiments. 2.3. Synthesis of L-PLA-100k by condensation of HO-PLA-OH and HOOC-PLA-OH The synthesis of linear PLA with Mw of ~100,000 (L-PLA-100k) was conducted as follows.

content: esis of L-PLA-100k by condensation of HO-PLA-OH and HOOC-PLA-OH The synthesis of linear PLA with Mw of ~100,000 (L-PLA-100k) was conducted as follows. A 50-mL screw-top test tube was flame-dried and then DMAP (0.035 mmol), 4-dimethylaminopyridinium p-toluene sul­fonate (DMAP⋅HOTs; 0.035 mmol), HO-PLA-OH (0.01 mmol), HOOCPLA-OH (0.015 mmol), CH2Cl2 (dehydrated with molecular sieves 3A, 2.8 mL), and 3A and 4A molecular sieves were successively added. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2 h. DIPCI (0.071 mmol) was added and then the mixture was stirred at 30 ◦C for16 h. After dilution with CH2Cl2 (1 mL), the solution was filtered through a poly­tetrafluoroethylene syringe filter (0.2-μm pore diameter) and then

content: K. Takada et al. Journal of Membrane Science 744 (2026) 125225 added dropwise into cold methanol to obtain a white precipitate. The precipitate was dried under vacuum at 40 ◦C for 5 h. The precipitate (475 mg) was dissolved in THF (25 mL) and then methanol (2.3 mL) was added dropwise. The precipitated fraction with high molecular weight was collected and then dried under vacuum at 40 ◦C for 6 h to give LPLA-100k. 2.4. Polymer characterization The obtained products were analyzed by 1H nuclear magnetic reso­nance (NMR) spectroscopy (400 MHz, JNM-ECZ400R, JEOL Ltd., Japan) using CDCl3 as the solvent. The number-average molecular weight (Mn), Mw, and dispersity (Mw/Mn) of PLA were determined by gel permeation chromatography (GPC). Absolute molecular weight was measured by a high-performance GPC system (Acquity APC, Waters, Milford, CT, USA) equipped with a refractive index (RI) detector (RI Detector, Waters), a multi-angle light scattering (MALS) detector (microDAWN, Wyatt Technology, Santa Barbara, CA, USA), and columns (XT45 (150 mm), XT200 (75 mm), and XT900 (75 mm); Waters) at 40 ◦C using THF as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 0.6 mL/min (GPC-MALS).

content: and columns (XT45 (150 mm), XT200 (75 mm), and XT900 (75 mm); Waters) at 40 ◦C using THF as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 0.6 mL/min (GPC-MALS). The branch structure of PLA and intrinsic viscosity of PLA solutions were analyzed using a GPC system (Alliance e2695, Waters) equipped with an RI detector (2414, Waters), MALS detector (DAWN 8, Wyatt Technology), viscometer (Visco Star, Wyatt Technology Co.), and col­umns (LB-803, LB-806 M ( * 2), Shoko Science Co., Ltd., Kanagawa, Japan) at 40 ◦C. DMF containing 10 mmol/L LiBr was used as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min (GPC-MALS-VISCO). 2.5. Membrane preparation Procedure of membrane preparation via NIPS is shown in Scheme 1. For membrane preparation via NIPS, the synthesized PLA samples were dissolved in NMP (good solvent) at 60 ◦C to prepare 20 wt% PLA solu­tions [12], which were then aged at 60 ◦C for 1 h. The solutions were cast onto glass plates (50 ◦C) using an applicator with a 200-μm gap [16]. The concentration of PLA solution was determined based on reports and our preliminary experimental screening (Fig. S1, Table S1). The glass plates were left to stand for 10 s and then immersed in distilled water (non-solvent) at 25 ◦C. After 30 min, the glass plates were transferred to fresh distilled water and immersed for more than 15 h.

Membranes are denoted by appending “-M” to the PLA sample names (e.g., membranes prepared from L-PLA-100k, 4S-PLA-100k, and 8S-PLA-100k are denoted as L-PLA-100k-M, 4S-PLA-100k-M, and 8S-PLA-100k-M, respectively).

content: 2.6. Membrane characterization 2.6.1. FE-SEM observation For surface observation, membranes were dried under vacuum and coated with osmium prior to analysis. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; JSM-7800F, JEOL Ltd.) was performed at an accelerating voltage of 1 kV. For cross-sectional observation, wet membranes were fractured in liquid nitrogen and then dried under vacuum. In this case, the samples were coated with platinum prior to analysis and FE-SEM (JSM-6610LA, JEOL Ltd.) was conducted at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. 2.6.2. Bulk porosity The bulk porosity (φ; %) of the membranes was calculated from the weights of the wet membrane (Wwet) and corresponding dry membrane (Wdry) using Eq. (1): φ = (Wwet -Wdry ρwater ) (Wwet -Wdry ρwater + Wdry ρPLA ) * 100(1) where ρwater is the density of water (1) and ρPLA is the density of PLA (1.25) [29]. 2.6.3. Water permeance Pure water permeance was measured by pressurizing distilled water on the top surface (in this study, top surface (the surface facing water during phase separation) had fine pores and bottom surface (the surface facing the glass substrate during phase separation) had macropores (see Scheme 1)). The transmembrane pressure was adjusted to 0.05–0.10 MPa by controlling the valve on the concentrated side. To ensure stable measurements, pure water permeance was measured 40 min after the beginning of pressurization. 2.6.4.

content: MWCO using PEGs with different molecular weights [30]. PEG 600, PEG 6,000, PEG 10,000, PEG 20,000, PEG 35,000, and PEG 100,000 (each 500 ppm) were dissolved in distilled water to obtain PEG aqueous feed solutions. The solutions were supplied to the membrane at 50–100 kPa under cross-flow filtration with stirring at 800 rpm. The rejection Ri (%) of PEG of molecular weight i was calculated using Eq. (2): Ri = 1 -(Cpi Cfi * 100)(2) where Cpi and Cfi are the concentrations of PEG of molecular weight i in the permeate and feed solutions, respectively, determined by GPC with RI detection (GPC-RI) [30]. Ri was determined for PEG with a molecular weight range of 6000–200,000. MWCO was defined as the molecular weight at which Ri reached 90 %. GPC measurements were performed at 40 ◦C using a GPC system (Nexera, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) equipped with an RI detector (RID-20A, Shimadzu) and two columns (OHpak SB-806 M HQ columns, Shoko Science Co., Ltd.) with an mobile phase flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The mobile phase was distilled water con­taining NaNO3 (50 mmol/L) and NaN3 (0.02 wt%). A calibration curve was prepared using Easi Vial PEG/PEO standards. 2.6.5. Thermal analysis Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) thermograms were measured using a DSC module (DSC6200, Hitachi High-Tech Co., Tokyo, Japan) under a nitrogen atmosphere (nitrogen flow rate of 40 mL/min) with a heating/cooling rate of 10 ◦C/min. Samples were subjected to a heating–cooling–heating cycle between 0 and 190 ◦C.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: ogen flow rate of 40 mL/min) with a heating/cooling rate of 10 ◦C/min. Samples were subjected to a heating–cooling–heating cycle between 0 and 190 ◦C. 2.6.6. Hydrolysis test Borate buffer (pH 9, 3 mL) was placed in screw-cap vials with a diameter of about 15 mm. Dried PLA membranes (10 * 10 mm) were immersed in the borate buffer. The samples were incubated at 65 ◦C for 20, 45, 88, 125, and 215 h for alkaline hydrolysis. Hydrolysis condition described above was set as an accelerated aging. The hydrolysis tem­perature was set at 65 ◦C, which is above Tg of PLA and thus promotes hydrolysis. At high pH values such as pH 14, hydrolysis occurs too rapidly (80 % of weight was lost in 3 h) and is unsuitable for analysis, therefore, pH 9 was selected. After hydrolysis, the membranes were collected by suction filtration using 1.0-μm membrane filters and then washed with water. The membranes were dried under vacuum for 5 h and then Mn was determined by GPC-MALS (see Section 2.4). The normalized Mn was calculated from the number-average molecular weight after hydrolysis (Mn after) and number-average molecular weight before hydrolysis (Mn before). Normalized Mn = Mn after Mn before * 100(3) 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Synthesis of PLA and evaluation of branch structure To investigate the effects of PLA molecular structure on membrane structure, performance, and hydrolysis behavior, PLAs with comparable Mw but different branch number were synthesized.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: molecular structure on membrane structure, performance, and hydrolysis behavior, PLAs with comparable Mw but different branch number were synthesized. The star-branched PLA used in this study consisted of PLA arm chains extending from a central initiator, and the target molecular weight of PLA was determined as follows. The entanglement molecular weight (Me) of PLA is approx­imately 9000 [31]. Consequently, for star-branched PLA, if the molec­ular weight of one arm is below Me, chain entanglement does not occur, resulting in a considerable decrease of mechanical strength. Therefore, Mn must be set so that one chain exceeds Me. Specifically, for eight-arm star-branched PLA, Mn needed to exceed 72,000 (9000 * eight arms) to ensure entanglement. For this reason, the lower limit of Mn was set as 72, 000. When eight-arm star-branched PLA with Mn = 42,000 (Mn of one arm was 5250) was used for membrane preparation, the resulting porous membrane had extremely low mechanical strength and collapsed upon handling. Similarly, for four-arm star-branched PLA, Mn = 36,000 (9000 * four arms) was set as the lower limit of Mn. In addition, because Mw correlates more strongly with physical properties than Mn, synthesis conditions were adjusted to produce PLAs with comparable Mw. In this study, because dispersity was controlled within a narrow range (Mw/Mn ≤1.2), when the lower limit of Mn was 72,000, the corresponding Mw was 86,400 (72,000 * 1.2).

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: e dispersity was controlled within a narrow range (Mw/Mn ≤1.2), when the lower limit of Mn was 72,000, the corresponding Mw was 86,400 (72,000 * 1.2). We decided to use PLA with a Mw of around 100,000 for membranes. Polymerization results are summarized in Table 1. A series of linear and branched PLAs (Run 1, 2, and 4–7) were synthesized via ringopening polymerization of lactide with organocatalyst (Fig. 1(a)). Because linear PLA with Mw = 100,000 was not obtained by this method, we attempted esterification of linear PLA with hydroxyl end groups and another linear PLA with a carboxylic acid terminal group to obtain the desired linear PLA with high Mw [32]. Specifically, linear PLA with hydroxyl groups at both ends (HO-PLA-OH, Run 1) was synthesized and then condensed with linear PLA with a carboxylic acid group at one end and a hydroxyl group at the other end (HOOC-PLA-OH, Run 2) ac­cording to Fig. 1(b) to increase Mw. Because the end functional groups are considered to affect the membrane structure and hydrolysis behavior, hydroxyl groups were used as end groups for all PLAs. Frac­tional precipitation was performed by dissolving the obtained crude PLA in THF and gradually adding methanol to precipitate the desired linear PLA with Mw = 97,000 and Mw/Mn = 1.20 (L-PLA-100k, Table 1, Run 3). Subsequently, four-arm star-branched PLA (4S-PLA-100k, Run 4) and eight-arm star-branched PLA (8S-PLA-100k, Run 5) with Mw comparable to that of L-PLA-100k (Run 3) were synthesized.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: nched PLA (4S-PLA-100k, Run 4) and eight-arm star-branched PLA (8S-PLA-100k, Run 5) with Mw comparable to that of L-PLA-100k (Run 3) were synthesized. 4S-PLA-100k and 8S-PLA-100k had Mw of 95,800 and 94,500, respectively, and narrow dispersity (Mw/Mn ≤1.20). 1H NMR spectroscopy measurements revealed that the catalyst was completely removed during purification (Fig. S2(a)–(c)). To evaluate the effect of Mw on membrane structure, performance, and hydrolysis behavior, four-arm star-branched PLAs with different Mw (4S-PLA-65k and 4S-PLA-50k, Run 6 and 7) were synthesized by changing the monomer/initiator ratio and polymeriza­tion time of ring-opening polymerization. 4S-PLA-65k and 4S-PLA-50k possessed Mw of 65,900 and 50,000, respectively, and had narrow dis­persity (Mw/Mn ≤1.20). The branch structures of L-PLA-100k, 4S-PLA-100k, and 8S-PLA100k were analyzed by GPC. A plot of the hydrodynamic radius of PLA molecules in solution against molecular weight M (Fig. 2(a)) indi­cated that the hydrodynamic radius decreased in the order of L-PLA100k > 4S-PLA-100k > 8S-PLA-100k. This means that the branching degree of PLA can be controlled by the functional group number of the initiator. This trend was also confirmed from the branching ratio g’M, which was calculated from the intrinsic viscosities of the branched and linear polymers ([η]br and [η]lin, respectively) using Eq. (4) (Fig. 2(b)) [33]. A smaller g’M value indicates a higher branch number. Fig.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: anched and linear polymers ([η]br and [η]lin, respectively) using Eq. (4) (Fig. 2(b)) [33]. A smaller g’M value indicates a higher branch number. Fig. 2(b) shows that the branch number increased in the order of L-PLA-100k <4S-PLA-100k < 8S-PLA-100k. These results demonstrate that the branch structure of PLA can be effectively controlled by varying the functional group number of the initiator. gʹ M =([η]br [η]lin ) M

3.2. Effect of PLA molecular structure on membrane structure In this section, we examine how PLA molecular structure and mo­lecular weight affect membrane structure. Regarding the membrane preparation conditions, the PLA solution concentration of 20 wt% was determined based on our preliminary experimental screening (Fig. S1and Table S1). At a lower concentration (10 wt%), the resulting mem­branes exhibited insufficient mechanical strength. In contrast, at a higher concentration (30 wt%), although the mechanical strength was improved, surface pores were hardly observable by SEM, making the

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

FE-SEM images of (a)–(c) PLA membranes with similar weight-average molecular weight Mw and different branch number, and (d)–(f) four-arm star-branched PLA membranes with different Mw. (b) and (d) show FE-SEM images of the same membrane. “Top surface” and “bottom surface” indicates the surfaces of non-solvent facing and glass plate facing side during phase separation, respectively (see Scheme 1). K. Takada et al. Journal of Membrane Science 744 (2026) 125225 images of the membrane top surfaces (Fig. 3(a2), (b2), (c2)) were analyzed. The results (Fig. 4(a) and Table 2) indicated that membranes with a higher branch number exhibit larger pore diameter in their top surface. In particular, the pore size distribution of 8S-PLA-100k-M with the largest branch number of the membranes showed an increase in the number of large pores with diameters exceeding 50 nm (Fig. 4(a)). Next, to investigate the effect of Mw on membrane structure, mem­branes were prepared from four-arm star-branched PLAs with different Mw. The structures of these membranes were observed by FE-SEM (Fig. 3(d)–(f), Fig. S3(d)–(f)). The top surface again contained the finest pores, and 4S-PLA-50k-M (Fig. 3(f2)) had the largest pore diameter among the membranes. Cross-sectional FE-SEM images (Fig. 3(d3), (e3), and (f3)) revealed the presence of finger-like macrovoid structures in all mem­branes. The image analysis results of top surface (the surfaces with fine pores) are presented in Fig. 4(b) and Table 2.

Membranes prepared from PLAs with lower Mw tended to have pores with larger diameters in their top surfaces. In particular, 4S-PLA-50k-M, which was the PLA with the lowest Mw, exhibited pores exceeding 100 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(f2) and in its pore size distribution in Fig. 4(b).

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: 3.3. Effect of PLA molecular structure on membrane performance Subsequently, we investigated how molecular structure and Mw affect membrane performance. The separation performance of mem­branes prepared from PLAs with similar Mw (~100,000) but different branch numbers was evaluated (Fig. 5(a), Table 2). Membranes with higher branch number exhibited larger MWCO values determined with PEG, indicating larger pore diameters than those of membranes with lower branch number. Next, the separation performance of membranes prepared from four-arm star-branched PLAs with different Mw was evaluated (Fig. 5(b)–Table 2). The results indicated that membranes composed of PLAs with lower Mw showed higher MWCO values and larger pore size than those of membranes prepared from PLAs with higher Mw. 4S-PLA-50k-M with the lowest Mw of the membranes rejec­ted <95 % of PEG with high molecular weight (above 100,000), indi­cating the presence of larger pores. The MWCO results displayed similar trends to those of the FE-SEM analysis (Fig. 4, Table 2), confirming that the pore structure of the membrane top surface strongly influences separation performance. The permeance of the PLA membranes (Table 2) differed by up to 20 times (4S-PLA-100k-M and 8S-PLA-100k-M). Therefore, we investigated the parameters of membrane structure that affect permeance. Note that diameter equivalent to MWCO [nm] was calculated by assigning MWCO to the PEG molecular weight (M) using Eq.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: structure that affect permeance. Note that diameter equivalent to MWCO [nm] was calculated by assigning MWCO to the PEG molecular weight (M) using Eq. (5) [34] which used for determining the PEG molecular diameter (D [nm]): D [nm] = 33.46 * 10-3 * M0.557(5) It should be noted that this pore diameter represents an apparent or effective value derived from MWCO, rather than a directly measured pore size. Parameters affecting permeance may include pore diameter, surface porosity, and bulk porosity as well as internal structural features such as tortuosity and effective flow-path length. The bulk porosity determined from water content was about 80 % for all membranes (Table 2), indicating that differences in permeance were not caused by bulk porosity. A clear relationship between permeance and the square of pore diameter (Fig. S4(a)) was observed. Furthermore, a strong rela­tionship was observed with top-surface porosity (Fig. S4(b)), suggesting that surface porosity is the dominant factor affecting permeance. A strong relationship between permeance and the product of the square of pore diameter and surface porosity (equivalent to the fourth power of pore diameter) was observed (Fig. 6(a)). This correlation is qualitatively consistent with a Hagen–Poiseuille-type dependence and can serve as an engineering-level description of the observed permeance trends.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: s qualitatively consistent with a Hagen–Poiseuille-type dependence and can serve as an engineering-level description of the observed permeance trends. How­ever, given that part of the pore diameter is derived from MWCO and NIPS membranes typically exhibit asymmetric structures such as fingerlike macrovoids and a dense skin layer, this relationship should not be interpreted as a deterministic transport mechanism. Instead, it should be regarded as an approximate or empirical correlation under the current dataset and structural assumptions. We next considered the factors causing differences in membrane structure based on molecular structure and molecular weight. Although crystallization can influence phase separation in some cases [35], no melting peaks (typically observed at 160–180 ◦C) were detected in the DSC thermograms of any PLA membranes (Fig. 6(b)). This result

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: Fig. 4. Pore diameter distribution (top surface) of PLA membranes obtained by FE-SEM image analysis. (a) PLA membranes with similar weight-average molecular weight Mw and different branch number. (b) Four-arm star-branched PLA membranes with different Mw. K. Takada et al. Journal of Membrane Science 744 (2026) 125225 indicates that all samples were amorphous and crystallization did not contribute to the observed structural changes. The small endothermic peak around 60 ◦C is attributed to enthalpy relaxation rather than melting (DSC thermograms of PLA membranes in the first cooling and second heating steps are shown in Fig. S5(a) and (b).) Next, we focused on the viscosity of PLA solution in phase separation. For the same Mw, an increase in the branch number of star-branched polymers lowers mo­lecular entanglement, leading to a decrease in intrinsic viscosity [η] [36]. In the case of the same branch number, a decrease of Mw leads to a decrease of [η] according to the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation: [η] = K⋅Maw(6) where K and a are constants determined by polymer structure, solvent, and temperature. Therefore, we focused on the solution viscosity as a factor that might affect the membrane structure. Although the results were obtained using DMF/LiBr as the mobile phase, GPC-MALS-VISCO measurements showed that [η] decreases with increasing PLA branch number or decreasing Mw (Fig. 6(c)). Both Mw and [η] changes followed the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation (Eq. (6)).

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: creases with increasing PLA branch number or decreasing Mw (Fig. 6(c)). Both Mw and [η] changes followed the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation (Eq. (6)). Since PLA solutions with a concentration of 20 wt% (solvent: NMP) was used in membrane preparation, the viscosity (η) of this solution was measured using a viscometer (Fig. 6(d)). Although Fig. 6(c) and (d) correspond to different PLA concentrations and represent the dilute and concentrated solution regimes, respectively, no significant differences were observed in the relationships between Mw, branch number and viscosity in these two regimes. It is considered that lower viscosity promotes faster molecular movement in the PLA solution and faster structure growth during phase separation [37,38], which leads to larger pore diameter and higher surface porosity. Fig. 6(e) indicates that membranes prepared from so­lutions with lower η values tend to have larger MWCO (i.e., larger pore diameter). A previous study on separation membranes using stereocomplex PLA indicated that increasing the stereocomplex ratio lowered the viscosity of the preparation solution [16]. Furthermore, membranes with larger pore diameter and higher water flux were ob­tained at lower viscosities [16], consistent with this study.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: ion [16]. Furthermore, membranes with larger pore diameter and higher water flux were ob­tained at lower viscosities [16], consistent with this study. Simulations exploring the relationship between phase separation structure and polymer relaxation time showed that shorter relaxation times lead to faster structural development and the formation of larger phase sepa­ration structures [37]. Relaxation time shortens as the viscosity of a polymer solution decreases [37]. As shown in Fig. 6(d), both an increase of branch number and decrease of Mw resulted in lower η, indicating that the relaxation time was also shortened. Therefore, it is considered that the shorter relaxation time caused by a higher branch number or lower Mw promoted development of phase separation structure, leading to the formation of larger pores and increased surface porosity. Overall, the observed changes in membrane structure with increasing branch num­ber or decreasing Mw can be attributed to decreased chain entanglement, which led to lower viscosity and shorter relaxation times during phase separation. The tensile test results (Table S2) showed that decreasing of the branch number or increasing of the Mw tended to result in higher tensile strength and higher elongation at break. In addition to this, 8S-PLA100k-M exhibited higher permeation performance compared with 4SPLA-50k-M (with a comparable MWCO), while the strength and elon­gation of 8S-PLA-100k-M were slightly higher than 4S-PLA-50k-M.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: rmance compared with 4SPLA-50k-M (with a comparable MWCO), while the strength and elon­gation of 8S-PLA-100k-M were slightly higher than 4S-PLA-50k-M. Therefore, when aiming to achieve large pore size and high perme­ation performance, increasing the branch number is considered to be more advantageous than reducing the molecular weight. In this study, it is necessary to distinguish the effects of branch number and Mw of PLA on membrane properties into direct and indirect contributions. For example, branch number and Mw directly change microscopic factors of molecular structure such as branching point density and chain packing density, thereby affecting chain entanglement and exerting a direct influence on the mechanical strength of the

On the other hand, the branch number and Mw also change solution viscosity and consequently affect phase separation behavior, which induces variations in the macroscopic factors: porous membrane structure. As a result, they are considered to indirectly influence me­chanical strength through changes in membrane structure. Similarly, the branch number and Mw are considered to indirectly affect membrane structure and the associated membrane performance. Thus, it should be noted that the branch number and Mw of PLA exert combined effects on the membrane properties. Regarding membrane structural design, it was clarified that the use of linear PLA leads to membranes with finer pore sizes and higher mechanical strength, whereas the use of eight-arm starbranched PLA results in membranes with larger pore sizes and higher water permeance. Table 3 provides a comparative summary of the performance of our PLA membranes with previously reported membranes.

[12] and Shen et al. [39]. The rejection performance of our membranes is higher than that of many reported membranes. content: 3.4. Effect of molecular structure on the hydrolysis of PLA membranes Finally, we investigated how molecular structure and Mw affect the hydrolysis behavior of PLA membranes. As a normal hydrolysis test, PLA membranes were immersed in phosphate buffer (pH 7) at 35 ◦C, and no change of Mn was observed. Then, as an accelerated hydrolysis test, membranes with different branch number were immersed in borate buffer (pH 9) at 65 ◦C and the change of Mn was monitored to investigate the effect of branch structure on hydrolysis. Because hydrolysis involves chemical reactions, Mn changes were analyzed according to a previous study [23]. Fig. 7(a) shows the time-dependent change of normalized Mn during hydrolysis (Not normalized Mn were shown in Fig. S6). Up to ~88 h, the order of normalized Mn from highest to lowest was L-PLA-100k-M > 8S-PLA-100k-M > 4S-PLA-100k-M; thereafter, the order changed to 8S-PLA-100k-M > L-PLA-100k-M > 4S4-PLA-100k-M. It is considered that the molecular chain density in star-branched polymers is higher near branch points (derived from the initiator) and decreases toward chain ends. Because the density near branch points increases with branch number, it is considered that water penetration and hydrolysis are inhibited more strongly near the branch points with increasing branch number.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: ranch number, it is considered that water penetration and hydrolysis are inhibited more strongly near the branch points with increasing branch number. Therefore, the fourand eight-arm star-­branched PLAs, which have a larger proportion of chain-end segments with lower density than that of linear PLA, are hydrolyzed faster than linear PLA initially. However, as hydrolysis proceeds, the eight-arm star-branched PLA, which is the most resistant to hydrolysis around the branch point, may begin to decompose more slowly than the other samples. To experimentally investigate the relationship between branch number and water penetration described above, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to evaluate water uptake (Fig. S7 and Table S3). To eliminate the influence of porous structures, nonporous PLA films (see supplementary information section 1.2.) were used, and they were immersed in borate buffer (pH 9) at 65 ◦C for 88h before FTIR measurement. Since the O–H stretching vibration origi­nating from absorbed water appears at 3654 cm-1 [41,42], the water uptake of each PLA sample was compared using the ratio of the O–H peak intensity to that of the methine groups of the PLA backbone (2882 cm-1). As shown in Fig. S7 and Table S3, the water uptake decreases with increasing branch number, suggesting that a higher density of branching points suppresses water penetration into the polymer matrix.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: ke decreases with increasing branch number, suggesting that a higher density of branching points suppresses water penetration into the polymer matrix. Therefore, PLA with higher branch number may suppress water pene­tration, and thus, hydrolysis proceeds more slowly. Meanwhile, chain-end segments with lower density in branched PLA may promote hydrolysis as described above. Therefore, the balance between densities of branching point and chain-end segments is important to promote hydrolysis. These findings suggest that at Mw ~100,000, four-arm star-branched PLA has a suitable balance of these to promote hydrolysis. Next, to evaluate the effect of Mw on hydrolysis behavior, the hy­drolysis of membranes composed of four-arm star-branched PLAs of different Mw was evaluated. Fig. 7(b) shows the time-dependent change of normalized Mn of the membranes. Across the hydrolysis period, membranes with higher initial molecular weight were hydrolyzed more rapidly. This may be attributed to the fact that for PLA with lower Mw, the ratio of branch points (derived from the initiator) to molecular weight is higher, resulting in higher local chain density near the branch points, which inhibits water penetration and hydrolysis. As shown in Fig. S7 and Table S3, the water uptake decreases with increasing Mw, indicating that an increased density of branching points suppresses water penetration into the polymer matrix.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: er uptake decreases with increasing Mw, indicating that an increased density of branching points suppresses water penetration into the polymer matrix. They also support that the balance between densities of branching point and chain-end segments is important to promote hydrolysis as described above. Therefore, for fourarm star-branched PLA, increasing Mw appears to promote hydrolysis. Crystalline domains hydrolyze more slowly than amorphous ones in biodegradable polymers [43]. DSC measurements (Fig. 6(b)) revealed no obvious melting peaks in the thermograms of the membranes, con­firming their amorphous state. Therefore, the difference in the hydro­lysis behavior of the membranes cannot be attributed to the presence of crystalline domains. In the same manner as discussed in Section 3.3, the effects of branch number and Mw on hydrolysis behavior were also classified into direct and indirect contributions. Branch number and Mw directly change factors of molecular structure such as branching point density and chain packing density, thereby directly influencing hydrolysis behavior. In addition, they also affect for membrane porous structure inducing change of surface area, which alters water uptake behavior as an indirect effect.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: In addition, they also affect for membrane porous structure inducing change of surface area, which alters water uptake behavior as an indirect effect. However, since the hydrolysis tests in this study were conducted above Tg of PLA, thermal shrinkage occurred at a very early stage (within 20 h), making it difficult to systematically evaluate the influence of membrane structure on hydrolysis behavior in the present work. From the viewpoint of molecular design for hydrolysis properties, a moderate branch number, such as that of four-arm PLA, is effective in accelerating hydrolysis, whereas linear and highly branched structures (eight-arm PLA) require longer hydrolysis times. As described above, it should be noted that the branch number and Mw of PLA exert combined effects on the membrane structure, membrane properties, and hydrolysis behaviors. For practical application of PLA membranes in water treatment, antifouling performance and long-term operational stability are impor­tant. Since PLA is hydrophobic polymer [44], blending or surface modification with hydrophilic polymers can enhance surface hydro­philicity and potentially improve antifouling performance. In addition, long-term operational test is necessary to investigate stability of

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: Fig. 7. Time-dependent change of normalized number-average molecular weight Mn during hydrolysis tests. (a) PLA membranes with similar Mw and different branch number. (b) Four-arm star-branched PLA membranes with different Mw. K. Takada et al. Journal of Membrane Science 744 (2026) 125225 blending, surface modification and membrane performance. In an eco­nomic perspective, commercial PLA resins are available at costs com­parable to or lower than those of conventional membrane polymers such as polysulfone and polyvinylidene fluoride, and PLA membranes can be prepared via NIPS process, suggesting potential for cost-competitive commercialization. 4. Conclusion PLAs with varied molecular structures were synthesized via ringopening polymerization with an organocatalyst. GPC measurements confirmed narrow molecular-weight distributions with controlled branch structure and molecular weight. Porous membranes prepared from the PLAs via NIPS demonstrated that both higher branch number and lower Mw led to increased MWCO. This behavior is attributed to decreased solution viscosity promoting development of phase separa­tion. The membranes exhibited relatively high MWCO compared with that of previously reported PLA membranes. Further improvement of casting conditions is expected to increase permeance and separation performance. In accelerated hydrolysis tests, the decrease ratio of molecular weight at 215 h was the largest for four-arm star-branched PLA.

citation.title: Effect of molecular structure on porous structure and performance of star-branched poly(lactic acid) ultrafiltration membranes

content: paration performance. In accelerated hydrolysis tests, the decrease ratio of molecular weight at 215 h was the largest for four-arm star-branched PLA. This suggests that moderate branch number accelerates hydrolysis, whereas excessive branch number inhibits water penetration and hydrolysis around the branch core. For the four-arm star-branched PLA mem­branes, higher Mw increased the hydrolysis rate, which is possibly caused by the greater fraction of rapidly hydrolyzable arm segments. Overall, this study demonstrates that both the performance and hydro­lizability of sustainable PLA UF membranes can be enhanced by con­trolling PLA branch structure and molecular weight.
